q: Your team is building a multi-tenant Azure Databricks platform with separate catalogs for dev, staging, and prod, and a third-party partner who will consume curated data. What naming convention strategy covers isolation, environment separation, and external sharing?
a: A well-structured naming convention encodes environment, ownership, and visibility at every level of the Unity Catalog hierarchy. For environment isolation, prefix catalogs with the environment token: `acme_dev`, `acme_stg`, `acme_prod`. For business domain isolation within an environment, add a domain segment: `acme_prod_finance`, `acme_prod_logistics`. Schemas inside a catalog should reflect data tier and lifecycle: `raw`, `curated`, `published`. For external-sharing catalogs, use a `_shared` or `_ext` suffix to make the intended audience explicit to both engineers and governance reviewers: `acme_prod_finance_shared`. Volumes holding landing files should use a `_landing` suffix and live in a dedicated schema. Consistency with these conventions lets Unity Catalog privilege grants, tag policies, and audit queries all target objects by name pattern rather than one-off enumeration.
fj: You should create one catalog per metastore to keep governance simple.
fs: A Unity Catalog metastore is a single account-level object; you cannot create more than one per Azure region per Databricks account. Catalogs are the correct isolation unit within a metastore—multiple catalogs per metastore is the designed pattern for environment and domain separation.

q: Walk through creating a Unity Catalog structure for a data platform: a prod catalog, a `raw` schema for landing files, a `curated` schema for cleaned tables, and a managed volume for incoming CSV files. What SQL commands and privilege requirements are needed?
a: You need the `CREATE CATALOG` privilege on the metastore to create the catalog, then `USE CATALOG` to set context. `CREATE SCHEMA` requires the privilege on the parent catalog. `CREATE VOLUME` requires the privilege on the parent schema. A managed volume stores files in the Databricks-managed storage location associated with the schema; an external volume would reference a pre-existing external location. After creation, you grant downstream roles the appropriate privileges using Unity Catalog GRANT syntax. The compute used to run DDL must be Databricks Runtime 13.3 LTS or later for volumes, and must use a Unity Catalog-compatible access mode (Shared or Single User cluster, or SQL warehouse).
fj: You can create volumes on any Databricks Runtime version as long as you use a Standard cluster.
fs: Volumes require Databricks Runtime 13.3 LTS or above, and the cluster must use a Unity Catalog-compatible access mode. Legacy Standard clusters without Unity Catalog governance do not expose volume paths.

code.label: Create catalog, schemas, and managed volume
lines: CREATE CATALOG IF NOT EXISTS acme_prod
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS raw
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS curated
CREATE VOLUME IF NOT EXISTS acme_prod.raw.csv_landing
COMMENT 'Drop zone for inbound CSV files';
-- Grant ingestion service principal read/write on volume

q: A security audit requires that raw landing tables in your Unity Catalog workspace are automatically purged after 90 days, while curated Delta tables are retained indefinitely. How do you implement data retention policies in Azure Databricks?
a: Azure Databricks data retention operates at two distinct layers that must be configured separately. First, Delta table file retention governs how long deleted data files are kept for time-travel; this is controlled by the table property `delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration` (default 7 days) and `delta.logRetentionDuration` (default 30 days). These are not lifecycle deletion policies—they affect `VACUUM` behavior. For true data lifecycle deletion, you implement a scheduled Lakeflow Job (or Databricks Workflow) that runs `DELETE FROM table WHERE event_date < current_date() - INTERVAL 90 DAYS` and then `VACUUM` to reclaim storage. Unity Catalog itself does not currently provide a built-in TTL policy that auto-deletes rows; the pattern is to enforce retention through a combination of table properties, scheduled jobs, and optionally Azure Lifecycle Management policies on the underlying ADLS Gen2 storage account for managed tables. For compliance use-cases, you can also apply column masking or row filters to restrict access to data beyond the retention window without physically deleting it immediately.
fj: Setting `delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration = '90 days'` will delete rows older than 90 days after 90 days.
fs: `delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration` controls how long *deleted* Parquet files are kept before `VACUUM` removes them—it does not cause rows to be deleted. To delete rows, you must explicitly run a `DELETE` statement or use a scheduled job.

code.label: Set retention properties and schedule deletion
lines: -- Shorten VACUUM window on raw table to 1 day for cost
-- Actual data lifecycle: delete rows older than 90 days
-- Reclaim storage (disable safety check for short retention)

q: How do you enable and query Unity Catalog audit logs in Azure Databricks, and what system table stores them?
a: Unity Catalog audit logs are captured in the system schema `system.access.audit`, which is part of the built-in system catalog (`system`). The schema must be enabled by an account admin through the Unity Catalog system schema enablement API (`/api/2.0/unity-catalog/metastores/{id}/systemschemas`), after which the table becomes queryable from any workspace attached to that metastore. Each row represents an auditable event—table reads, privilege grants, login events, etc.—and includes fields such as `event_time`, `action_name`, `user_identity`, `service_name` (e.g., `unityCatalog`), `request_params`, and `response`. Unity Catalog events appear with `service_name = 'unityCatalog'` and `workspace_id = 0` because they are account-level events; the originating workspace is found in `request_params.workspace_id`. Azure Databricks retains logs in this table for up to one year. You can build dashboards on top of these tables using SQL warehouses or schedule audit-report jobs.
fj: Audit logs must be exported to Azure Monitor or a Log Analytics Workspace before you can query them in Databricks.
fs: While you can forward diagnostic logs to Azure Monitor for external SIEM integration, Unity Catalog natively exposes audit data through the `system.access.audit` system table that is directly queryable with SQL inside Databricks—no external export is required for in-platform querying.

q: You need to share a Delta table with a partner company running their own Databricks workspace, and with an external analytics team using non-Databricks tools. Design a secure Delta Sharing strategy that covers both consumers.
a: Delta Sharing has two protocols: Databricks-to-Databricks (D2D) and open sharing. For the partner Databricks workspace, use D2D: the provider creates a share in their Unity Catalog metastore (`CREATE SHARE`), adds the table to it (`ALTER SHARE … ADD TABLE`), then creates a recipient referencing the partner's Databricks sharing identifier (`CREATE RECIPIENT … USING ID 'databricks://<account-id>'`). The partner's account admin accepts the share in their own Unity Catalog, which creates a foreign catalog they query directly. For the external non-Databricks team, use open sharing: create a separate recipient without a Databricks identifier, which generates a bearer-token activation link. Deliver the link over a secure channel; the recipient downloads a credential file containing a bearer token. Set the token lifetime to the shortest acceptable window (configure `delta.sharing.recipientTokenTtlSeconds` on the metastore). Apply IP access lists to the recipient to lock down which network CIDR ranges can authenticate. Grant only the minimum table columns and apply row filters or column masks before adding to the share to limit blast radius. Audit sharing access via `system.access.audit` filtering on `service_name = 'deltaSharingMosaic'`.
fj: To share with an external non-Databricks team, you must copy the Delta files to a public storage container and give them the SAS URL.
fs: Copying files to a public container bypasses all Unity Catalog governance, audit logging, and access revocation. Delta Sharing open protocol is specifically designed for this use-case: it provides a governed, auditable, and revocable way to share live Delta tables with non-Databricks consumers without exposing raw storage credentials.

code.label: Create share, add table, and configure open-sharing recipient
lines: COMMENT 'Curated sales data shared with external partners';
-- Add a table (only specific columns via table alias)
COMMENT 'External BI team — token expires in 7 days';
-- Retrieve the activation link to send securely

q: A raw data zone needs to ingest from three sources: nightly CSV drops in ADLS Gen2, real-time CDC from SQL Server, and a weekly Salesforce CRM export. Design the ingestion logic covering extraction type and file type for each source.
a: Each source has a different extraction type and optimal file/format strategy. For ADLS Gen2 CSV drops: use Auto Loader (`cloudFiles` format) in Databricks with a structured streaming job that reads new CSV files incrementally as they land. Auto Loader uses file-notification mode (Azure Event Grid + queue) for low-latency discovery at scale. Land into a Delta table in the raw schema with `mergeSchema = true` to tolerate column additions. For SQL Server CDC: use Lakeflow Connect's managed SQL Server connector, which uses CDC log-based capture to extract only changed rows on each run after the full initial load. The output is a Delta table with CDC metadata columns (`_change_type`, `_commit_timestamp`). This avoids expensive full-table scans. For Salesforce CRM: use Lakeflow Connect's managed Salesforce connector, which uses Salesforce Bulk API 2.0 for initial load and incremental API for delta updates. The extraction type is batch-incremental, and the output is Delta. Choosing Delta as the unified landing format across all three sources simplifies downstream schema-on-read, MERGE operations, and time-travel queries.
fj: For SQL Server CDC, the best approach is a full-table SELECT on a schedule using ADF Copy Activity to Parquet files.
fs: Full-table SELECT scans create high load on the source, produce large files, and require downstream logic to detect what changed. CDC via Lakeflow Connect reads only the transaction log changes, minimizing source impact and enabling row-level lineage with `_change_type` metadata.

q: When would you choose Azure Data Factory over Lakeflow Connect for ingesting data into Azure Databricks, and vice versa?
a: Lakeflow Connect is the preferred choice when the source is a supported SaaS app (Salesforce, Workday, ServiceNow) or a relational database connector (SQL Server, PostgreSQL) where you want CDC-based incremental ingestion, serverless execution, native Unity Catalog lineage, and zero connector infrastructure management. It is tightly integrated with Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines (formerly DLT) and governed entirely within Databricks. Azure Data Factory (ADF) is the better choice when orchestrating cross-system pipelines that span many non-Databricks services—Azure Synapse, Azure SQL Database, Blob Storage, on-premises sources via self-hosted integration runtime, SAP, or 90+ ADF connectors not yet in Lakeflow Connect. ADF excels at triggering Databricks notebooks or jobs as one step in a broader enterprise workflow, managing complex dependency chains, or when your organization already has ADF expertise and governance. In practice, many enterprise architectures use both: ADF for system-level orchestration and cross-service data movement, Lakeflow for heavy Spark-based transformation and CDC ingestion within the Databricks lakehouse.
fj: Lakeflow Connect replaces Azure Data Factory entirely for Azure Databricks shops.
fs: ADF supports 90+ connectors including SAP, on-premises sources via self-hosted IR, and deep Azure service integration that Lakeflow Connect does not yet cover. Most enterprise teams run both in a complementary architecture rather than a full replacement.

q: Your team is landing raw data and must decide whether to store it as CSV, Parquet, Delta Lake, or Apache Iceberg. Walk through the decision criteria for each format in an Azure Databricks context.
a: CSV is human-readable and universally supported but lacks schema enforcement, compression efficiency, and predicate pushdown—it is appropriate only as an ephemeral landing format in a volume before conversion, or for small reference files exchanged with non-technical partners. Parquet is a columnar binary format offering good compression and predicate pushdown, and is appropriate when the data must remain readable by external tools (Azure Synapse, Power BI direct query, Spark outside Databricks) without a table format layer. Delta Lake is the Databricks default and should be chosen for all mutable, queryable tables within the lakehouse: it adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, time-travel, MERGE support, Change Data Feed (CDC), Z-order clustering, and Unity Catalog governance. Delta is backed by Parquet files plus a `_delta_log` transaction log. Apache Iceberg should be chosen when interoperability with non-Databricks engines is mandatory and you need strong schema evolution semantics—Iceberg's manifest-list design offers fine-grained partition evolution without rewrites. Databricks supports reading and writing Iceberg via UniForm, which simultaneously writes Delta and Iceberg metadata so the same table is consumable by both ecosystems. JSON is suitable only as a landing format for semi-structured or schema-less data in volumes before parsing into a structured Delta table.
fj: Delta and Parquet are equivalent choices; Delta just adds a UI layer on top of Parquet files.
fs: Delta adds a transaction log (`_delta_log`) that provides ACID guarantees, MERGE semantics, schema evolution, time-travel, and CDF on top of Parquet. These are architectural capabilities, not UI features. Without the transaction log, concurrent writes can corrupt a Parquet table and there is no rollback or time-travel.

q: Show how to create a Lakeflow Connect ingestion pipeline for a SQL Server source using CDC, and explain the batch vs. streaming modes available.
a: Lakeflow Connect managed connectors create a Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipeline internally. For SQL Server, CDC mode captures row-level inserts, updates, and deletes from the SQL Server transaction log via the connector, eliminating full-table scans. The pipeline can run in triggered mode (batch-like: runs to completion when manually triggered or scheduled) or continuous mode (streaming: keeps the pipeline running and continuously applies CDC changes as they arrive). Triggered mode is appropriate for latency-tolerant workloads (hourly or daily refreshes), while continuous mode targets near-real-time use-cases. The pipeline outputs Delta tables with standard CDC metadata columns. After initial setup through the Databricks UI or API, the pipeline is managed as a Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipeline and can be monitored in the pipeline event log.
fj: Lakeflow Connect pipelines run on classic interactive clusters and must be manually triggered each time.
fs: Lakeflow Connect managed connectors run on serverless compute managed by Databricks—you do not provision or manage clusters. They support both triggered (batch) and continuous (streaming) execution modes and integrate with scheduled Lakeflow Jobs for automation.

code.label: Create Lakeflow Connect SQL Server CDC pipeline via REST API

q: What is a Declarative Automation Bundle (formerly Databricks Asset Bundle) and what files must a valid bundle contain?
a: A Declarative Automation Bundle (DAB), renamed from Databricks Asset Bundles on March 16 2026, is a project packaging standard that treats Databricks resources—jobs, pipelines, notebooks, dashboards, models—as version-controlled source files alongside application code. A valid bundle requires exactly one `databricks.yml` file at the project root; this is the primary configuration file that declares the bundle name, workspace targets (dev/staging/prod), and resource definitions. The `databricks.yml` can reference additional YAML files via an `include` mapping to keep configuration modular. Resource definitions (jobs, pipelines) live under the `resources` key. Target-specific overrides use the `targets` key with environment-specific workspace host, cluster policies, and variable values. The Databricks CLI (v0.218.0+) drives the lifecycle: `databricks bundle validate` checks YAML correctness and resolves references, `databricks bundle deploy --target prod` deploys resources to the target workspace, and `databricks bundle run <resource>` executes a job or pipeline. Bundles are designed to integrate with CI/CD pipelines (Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions) for automated deployment across environments.
fj: Each Databricks job or pipeline must have its own separate databricks.yml file.
fs: A bundle has exactly one `databricks.yml` at the root. Multiple resources (jobs, pipelines, models) are all declared within that single file or in referenced YAML files. Having multiple root `databricks.yml` files in the same bundle is not valid.

q: Walk through the Databricks CLI commands to validate and deploy a Declarative Automation Bundle to a production target.
a: The deployment workflow using the Databricks CLI (v0.218.0+) follows a validate-deploy-run sequence. First authenticate: configure the CLI with a Databricks OAuth or PAT profile for the target workspace (`databricks configure --profile prod`). Then validate the bundle to catch YAML errors, unresolved variable references, and permission issues without making any changes to the remote workspace (`databricks bundle validate --target prod --profile prod`). After a clean validation, deploy to create or update all declared resources in the target workspace (`databricks bundle deploy --target prod --profile prod`). This command uploads source artifacts, creates or updates jobs/pipelines via the Databricks REST API, and prints a deployment summary. To trigger a job post-deploy: `databricks bundle run daily_ingestion --target prod --profile prod`. In a CI/CD pipeline (e.g., Azure DevOps), these three commands map to build, release, and optional smoke-test stages, with the profile credentials injected as environment variables (`DATABRICKS_HOST`, `DATABRICKS_TOKEN`) rather than stored in a config file.
fj: You must manually create the jobs in the Databricks UI after deploying the bundle, because the CLI only uploads notebooks.
fs: `databricks bundle deploy` uses the Databricks Jobs and Pipelines REST APIs to create or update all resources declared in the `databricks.yml`—jobs, pipelines, dashboards, and model serving endpoints—automatically. You do not need to create resources manually in the UI.

code.label: Validate, deploy, and run a bundle via CLI
lines: # Configure OAuth profile for production workspace
# Deploy all resources declared in databricks.yml
# CI/CD: use env vars instead of --profile

q: A Lakeflow Job with five tasks failed partway through on the third task. What steps do you take to diagnose the failure and execute a repair run that skips already-successful tasks?
a: Start by navigating to the Jobs UI and clicking the failed run's timestamp link in the Runs tab. The Job run details page shows all five tasks color-coded: green (succeeded), red (failed), and grey (skipped/not-started). Hover over the red task to see the error message snippet and a link to the full task run logs. Click View logs to inspect the driver stdout, stderr, and Spark event log for root cause—common causes include data quality failures, schema mismatches, missing files, insufficient cluster memory (OOM), or misconfigured parameters. If the failure is a transient issue (network blip, OOM on an undersized cluster), click Repair run: this opens the Repair job run dialog listing only the failed task and any downstream tasks that depend on it. Confirm to rerun—already-succeeded tasks are not re-executed, saving time and compute cost. If the failure requires a config change, click Edit task inside the dialog, update the task (e.g., increase cluster memory, fix notebook path), save, then trigger the repair. After the repair completes, the matrix view adds a new column showing the repaired run, and the combined run is considered successful only when all tasks show green. You can also invoke a repair programmatically via the Jobs REST API endpoint `POST /api/2.1/jobs/runs/repair` with the `run_id` and `rerun_tasks` list.
fj: To fix a failed multi-task job, you must cancel the failed run and create a brand-new full job run from scratch.
fs: Lakeflow Jobs supports repair runs natively: only failed tasks and their downstream dependents are re-executed, while succeeded tasks are preserved. This avoids redundant reprocessing and reduces recovery time significantly on complex multi-task pipelines.

code.label: Trigger a repair run via REST API
lines: RUN_ID    = 123456   # ID of the failed run
# Identify failed tasks from the run status
# Initiate repair run for failed tasks only
